words.cultural_note: This phrase carries deep meaning in Yoruba culture — welcoming someone into your space is considered a sacred act of hospitality, not just a polite greeting.
Àṣà is one of the most important concepts in Yoruba life. It refers not just to customs but to the inherited wisdom passed from ancestors to living people.
The most important human quality in Yoruba ethics. Ìwà pẹ̀lẹ́ (gentle character) is the highest ideal — above wealth or beauty
Literally 'head,' but refers to your spiritual destiny chosen before birth. Your Orí is your inner guide and protector.
Often translated as 'power' or 'so be it,' but it means the life force running through everything — people, words, nature. Speaking something with àṣẹ makes it real.
The highest compliment in Yoruba society — a person of good breeding, moral depth, and dignity; being called this by an elder is worth more than any title.
The costumed embodiment of returned ancestors during festivals — not a symbol but a living presence, believed to genuinely cross the boundary between the dead and the living.
Extends far beyond the nuclear family to encompass all who claim you — your safety net, your identity, and your obligation; in Yoruba life, you are never truly alone if you have ẹbí.
A vast oral corpus of poetry, history, ethics, and medicine consulted through divination — so significant UNESCO recognised it as intangible cultural heritage of humanity in 2005.
The distant, perfect creator in Yoruba cosmology — too vast for direct approach, which is why the Òrìṣà (deities) exist as intermediaries between humans and the divine.
